topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notification, stark white against the dark screen of my laptop, felt like a physical blow. I reread the fine print, my fingers trembling slightly as they traced the words detailing the scholarship disbursement. It was a maze of names, and my own name was conspicuously absent from the designated recipient list. A coldness seeped into my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight that made it hard to draw a full breath. I slowly closed the laptop, the soft click echoing unnervingly in the quiet dorm room. I stood by the window, watching the campus lights blur through the evening mist, the world outside seeming distant and muted. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, a knot of unexpressed turmoil twisting beneath my skin.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the interview, searching for a misinterpretation, a slip of the tongue that could explain this impossible discrepancy. The silence in my small apartment was oppressive, amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears. When I finally found the confirmation email, the reality hit me with a dull, heavy thud. The funds, the entire future I had meticulously planned around this opportunity, belonged to someone else entirely. I sank onto the edge of my bed, my shoulders slumping forward as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot path down my cheek, and I scrubbed at it with the heel of my hand, the gesture weak and utterly defeated.

[story 3]

"No," I whispered to the empty room, the sound barely audible. I walked back to the administrative office, my steps slow and heavy, each footfall seeming to drag through thick water. When the registrar finally confirmed my dreadful suspicion, the air seemed to thin around me. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a profound emptiness, a hollow ache where hope used to reside. I stared at the stack of papers on the desk, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. I picked up my backpack, the weight suddenly unbearable, and walked out into the hallway, feeling utterly adrift, like a ship without a rudder in a vast, indifferent ocean.
